title: The Incidence of Student Loan Subsidies: Evidence from the PLUS Program
abstract: How much do students benefit from student loan subsidies? We investigate this question, exploiting a natural experiment: a demand shock due to the 2011 tightening of credit standards in the PLUS program. We first establish that the Bennett hypothesis is best explained by colleges charging large markups over their marginal costs, rather than by advantageous selection. Then we use our results to estimate that students plausibly capture less than 60 cents of each dollar of resources expended on loan subsidies. We discuss alternative approaches that would more directly benefit students.

author.Mahyar Kargar: Gies College of Business,University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign

article.1. Theory:
JEL G28 - Government Policy and Regulation G5 - Household Finance H22 - Incidence I22 - Educational Finance; Financial Aid I23 - Higher Education; Research Institutions Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Student loan subsidies have been a major policy response to the growth of college tuition in the United States. These programs help to expand enrollment, but also have the perverse effect of further increasing tuition, a pattern known as the “Bennett hypothesis.” In turn, higher tuition feeds back into greater student debt burdens that affect later life decisions.1These patterns have sparked debate as to the incidence of subsidized student loan programs: to what extent are they wasted, by failing to reduce the cost of college for the students who rely on them?

One might think that evidence of the Bennett hypothesis already answers this question. But a pass-through effect of a subsidy on prices does not reveal the incidence of that subsidy. For example, if students who enroll due to receiving subsidized loans are more expensive to educate, then tuition must rise in response to aid, even if students capture all of the benefit. For this reason, empirical research in other markets has focused on disentangling market power from selection as the explanation for empirical pass-through effects (e.g.,Cabral, Geruso, and Mahoney 2018).

In the same spirit, we disentangle competing explanations for the Bennett hypothesis. We exploit an inward demand shock to identify the costs of enrolling students who were at the margin of qualifying for a specific loan program. By comparing these costs with the prices that those students paid, we establish that the Bennett hypothesis is best explained by colleges charging very large markups over marginal costs. We use these findings to estimate that students capture less than 60 cents of each dollar in loan subsidies, confirming the concerns raised by the Bennett hypothesis.

To motivate this analysis, and illustrate the identification challenges involved, we first present a simple analysis of colleges’ revenues and spending. While college revenues have grown steadily, both in the aggregate and per student, college spending has grown at roughly the same pace. In ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions, college spending appears to be convex in enrollment, suggesting that average costs have risen along with aggregate growth in college attendance.

Based on these patterns, one might think that colleges raise tuition simply to keep pace with their costs. Indeed, prior estimates of markups in higher education are quite small (Epple, Romano, and Sieg 2006). A selection-based model can fit all of these patterns, and under this model the Bennett hypothesis does not necessarily imply any concerns about subsidy incidence. On the contrary, this model undercuts the case for providing subsidies in the first place: The students who use them to attend already pay less for college than it costs to educate them.

However, all the above evidence is based on average costs. To test for selection effects, one needs to identify and measuremarginalcosts (Einav and Finkelstein 2011). One strategy to do so is to find a demand shifter that traces out the cost curve, while avoiding simultaneity concerns. While this approach is standard in research on industrial organization, it has not been pursued to date in the context of higher education.

To fill this gap, we exploit an inward demand shock that occurred in October 2011, because of the tightening of credit standards for Parent Loans to Undergraduate Students (PLUS loans). While consolidating the older Federal Family Education Loan (FFEL) and newer Direct Loan (DL) origination programs, the Department of Education discovered that they had used inconsistent interpretations of the term “adverse credit history,” which is a disqualifying condition for PLUS loans. It harmonized the two programs using the stricter definition.

This change was expected to have little real effect but instead disqualified many parents from PLUS loan eligibility. Colleges and commentators blamed the change for enrollment declines, revenue shortfalls, and budget cuts. There were multiple potential mechanisms for these effects: Some disqualified parents would have been unable to find other financing. Others could have used alternative sources of financing, but would have been discouraged from doing so by the PLUS denial. Through either mechanism, the standards tightening constituted a demand shock that we can use to address the endogeneity concerns with our earlier OLS results.

To validate the importance of this event, we exploit data on enrollments, financial aid, and expenditures for private colleges in the United States offering federal loans. We calculate the fraction of aid recipients that came from low-income families as of 2010, and use this fraction as our cross-sectional measure of treatment exposure. Starting with the 2011–2012 school year, the growth rates of both PLUS loans and enrollment fell sharply for colleges with more low-income students.

We find evidence of the Bennett hypothesis in response to this shock: The growth rate in advertised tuition slowed, in relative terms, at colleges more affected by the standards tightening. This evidence implies that the availability of PLUS loans increases the price of college. It also complements prior studies, in that we study a decrease rather than an increase in aid.

Next, we test whether increasing average costs can explain the Bennett hypothesis, as suggested by our descriptive results. As expected, we find that the standards tightening caused a decrease in college spending. However, this decrease is far less than proportional to the decrease in enrollment. Put differently, the students who chose not to enroll after being disqualified for PLUS loans would have decreased, not increased, their colleges’ average costs. This pattern is inconsistent with a selection-based explanation of the Bennett hypothesis.

Instead, the explanation most consistent with the data is that colleges charge very large markups over marginal costs. Quantitatively, we estimate that, at the median private college, a marginal student would generate about $23,000 in revenue, while imposing costs of only $14,000. From this we calculate that roughly 40|$\%$|of a marginal student’s cost of attendance at the typical college represents a markup over the cost that student would impose on the college.

Large markups support the market power explanation for the Bennett hypothesis, and they imply that the benefit from a subsidy does not fully flow to consumers.Weyl and Fabinger (2013)provide a formula to quantify this effect in a general economic framework, with the size of markups as a critical input. We calibrate their formula using our estimates of colleges’ markups, and plausible values of other parameters drawn from the literature. From this calculation we conclude that students likely capture less than $0.60 of each dollar of public funds spent on loan subsidies.

We conclude by discussing two important questions: First, who benefits from loan subsidies, if not the student borrower? Most colleges do not have any explicit category of residual claimants. However, economic profits likely flow through to nonstudent stakeholders, especially employees with market power in their own labor markets. Second, are there better approaches to subsidizing higher education? We present a simple model in which an entry subsidy is superior to a loan program, and we discuss how this might look in practice.

In sum, we find that the pass-through effect captured by the Bennett hypothesis can be explained by colleges charging large markups over their marginal costs. Our results shed light on the underlying causes of the Bennett hypothesis, contrast prior estimates of small markups in higher education, and provide guidance as to why higher education subsidies may fail to have their intended effect, and what alternatives are possible.

Our paper contributes to a growing literature on the real effects of student loans. Several prior studies demonstrate the Bennett hypothesis, includingLucca, Nadauld, and Shen (2018),Turner (2017),Singell and Stone (2007), andRizzo and Ehrenberg (2003). We confirm this effect in our setting, but our main contribution is to investigate what model best explains it (selection or market power), and what are its policy implications. On the topic of college spending,Deming and Walters (2017)show that spending increases are more effective than than price decreases in expanding enrollment and promoting degree completion.

Compared to the literature in economics and finance, our paper is also novel for its focus on the PLUS program, as most papers in this literature study the Stafford or Pell Grant programs. To our knowledge, the only prior economics paper on PLUS loans isBhole (2017), who focused on the graduate PLUS program (whereas we focus on the undergraduate program). In the sociology literature,Cepa (2021)shows that PLUS loans predict higher parental debt levels across the income distribution, while providing limited benefits in terms of degree attainment.

Another related literature investigates the market structure and economics of higher education. These papers generally take a structural approach.Epple, Romano, and Sieg (2006)estimate a model of college admissions, and find markups of less than 3|$\%$|⁠, much smaller than our estimates (see further discussion below). Their framework is also employed inGordon and Hedlund (2016)andEpple et al. (2017), and other structural approaches are taken inFu (2014),Lau (2015), andFillmore (2016). In contrast, we take a reduced-form approach to estimating marginal costs and markups, and we study incidence using a “sufficient statistic” approach that is largely agnostic about the structural details of the market in question (Weyl and Fabinger 2013).

More broadly, our paper fits into a growing literature that investigates the sources and consequences of growing markups across various industries (e.g.Gutiérrez and Philippon 2017,Hall 2018).Berry, Gaynor, and Scott Morton (2019)review this literature, and emphasize the importance and the difficulty of identifying marginal costs.

Because the tightening of PLUS credit standards involved a redefinition of the term “adverse credit history,” our paper also contributes to a growing literature in household finance documenting the real impact of credit histories. Examples includeGonzález-Uribe and Osorio (2014),Bos and Nakamura (2014),Bos, Breza, and Liberman (2018), andGarmaise and Natividad (2017).

Important questions that we cannot assess include the optimality of a student’s decision to borrow, or the impact of student loan burdens and repayments on later life outcomes. SeeChakrabarti et al. (2017),Krishnan and Wang (2019),Mueller and Yannelis (2019),Cox (2019),Di Maggio, Kalda, and Yao (2019),Catherine and Yannelis (2020),Ebrahimian (2020), andGoodman, Isen, and Yannelis (2021)for examples in these directions.
To fix ideas, we briefly summarize and compare two competing models for the Bennett hypothesis. AppendixCjustifies the claims made in this section more rigorously.2

The first model that we consider is based on market power. In this model, there is imperfect competition in the market for higher education. Each student imposes the same marginal cost on the college they attend, which may include, for example, the marginal necessary expenditures on faculty compensation, facilities, academic support, and career services. The college charges a positive markup over that cost, and this markup is sustained due to the lack of free entry in an imperfectly competitive market.

The second model that we consider is based on selection. In this model, higher education is competitive, in the sense that tuition equals the average cost of enrolling a student. However, that cost is now heterogeneous. We assume that colleges cannot observe a student’s cost ex ante, and thus cannot price discriminate on this basis.3We further assume that students who require less financial aid to cover the costs of college also impose lower marginal costs on the school after they enroll; for example, they may require less academic support or counseling after enrolling, or less investment in marketing to attract them to enroll in the first place. Then, as financial aid expands, the new students who enroll are more costly than the students who would have enrolled regardless. In other words, selection is advantageous (e.g.,Einav and Finkelstein 2011).

The Bennett hypothesis holds in both models, but for different reasons. In the first model, market power allows incumbent colleges to absorb a subsidy partially by increasing prices, rather than by increasing enrollment. In the second model, average costs rise with enrollment, forcing colleges to charge more simply to keep tuition equal to average cost.

As a result, the policy implications of the two models are very different. In the first, college enrollment is suboptimally low, because of the deadweight loss of market power, so there is a clear case for subsidizing enrollment. The main question is how to enact this subsidy, while minimizing the tendency for it to increase prices, which blunts its intended impact.

In the second model, this concern does not apply: colleges set prices equal to average costs at all times, so there is no concern that a subsidy would fail to flow through to benefit students. If anything, the model might argue against providing such a subsidy in the first place, as the students who would use it to enroll in college pay even less than the cost of their education.

Because of the different policy implications of these two models, one must disentangle them before deciding how to react to evidence of the Bennett hypothesis. Next, we consider the kinds of evidence that could be used for this purpose.

Either model seems plausible a priori. One the one hand, market power seems likely to be significant in higher education because of the substantial time and resources required to build a college’s reputation. Market power could explain why college rankings and enrollments are so static over long periods of time. On the other hand, it also seems reasonable that a student who enrolls only due to expanded financial aid will require greater resources from the college, both to attract them to campus and to support them through graduation. Increasing marginal costs could explain why a growing share of college spending goes to student services and administrative categories, such as academic and institutional support.4

However, we can highlight two key empirical implications that distinguish the two models:

First, the market power model predicts that the marginal student pays a price far above the cost of educating them, while the selection model predicts that if anything they pay less. We can investigate this by regressing revenues and spending on enrollment, and comparing the magnitudes of the coefficients. Under the selection model the two coefficients should be similar, while under the market power model the revenue coefficient should be much greater.

Second, the market power model predicts that average costs are decreasing with enrollment, while the selection model predicts they are increasing. We can investigate this by regressing the logarithm of expenditures on the logarithm of enrollment. Under the market power model, the estimated coefficient should be less than one, while under the selection model, it should be greater than one.

In the remainder of the paper, we implement these regressions, highlight the identification concerns that arise with them, and then develop a strategy to address those concerns.
article.2. Data:
We use two primary sources, both published by the Department of Education (DOE). The first data source is the annual Title IV Program Volume Reports. These provide the number of recipients and loan origination volume for each school participating in Title IV aid, separated by loan program. The second data source is the Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System (IPEDS). Every college whose students are eligible for Title IV aid must annually complete a series of surveys that are published on IPEDS. The survey components include enrollment, school finances, and student financial aid. AppendixAdiscusses the details of merging the data sets.

Both IPEDS and the Title IV reports are timed by award year. The Department of Education defines an award year as the school year for which financial aid is used to fund a student’s education. Generally, this is the 12-month period that begins on July 1 of a given year and ends on June 30 of the following year. Hence, in all of the following analysis, year|$t$|is defined as the award year beginning on July 1 of calendar year|$t$|and ending on June 30 of calendar year|$t+1$|⁠.

We restrict the sample to private schools, because public schools are partially regulated and likely to reflect different economic forces.5We exclude schools that enrolled fewer than one thousand students during 2010, which account for one-third of sample colleges but less than 10|$\%$|of total enrollment. To implement our natural experiment, we require that each school appear in the Title IV data, and have nonmissing data on enrollment, expenses, and advertised tuition. Finally, we retain only schools that, after applying the above filters, appear in the data for at least 2010, 2011, and 2012. After these steps, our sample consists of 1,029 colleges.Table 1displays summary statistics for the schools in our sample.

The sample consists of data on 1,005 distinct school systems from 2002 to 2018. The data are from IPEDS and Title IV reports, and the sample construction is described in Section 2 . The final row in the table is the treatment exposure proxy for the natural experiment, |$\text{F}_{i,2010}$|⁠ , as described in Section 5.1 . “UFTEs” is an abbreviation for undergraduate FTEs, defined as hours of undergraduate instruction divided by 30.

Enrollment is measured in multiple ways in IPEDS. We will focus on undergraduate full-time equivalents (FTEs), defined as the total credit hours of undergraduate instruction, divided by 30 (based on the standard 30 credit hours in a full-time load), thus taking into account differences in enrollment across students. This focus stays close to the layout of the IPEDS Finance form, which ends with a summary of revenues and expenses per FTE for the college-year being reported.

InFigure 1, we compare aggregate spending by colleges in our sample with their aggregate revenues, excluding investment income.6We confirm that college revenues rapidly grew from 2002 to 2018. However, this growth was closely tracked by increases in spending, a pattern that may be less widely appreciated. InTable 1, the median annual growth rate of total spending (3.9|$\%$|⁠) slightly exceeds that of noninvestment revenues (3.6|$\%$|⁠). These trends suggest that the selection model from Section1deserves consideration as a potential explanation for the Bennett hypothesis.

The first two quantities inFigure 2capture different ways of thinking about the student-level “price” of college. Between the two, we can highlight several advantages of measuring price through realized revenues, rather than advertised tuition. First, realized revenues include sources other than tuition alone, such as residence and dining facilities, a valid component of the student’s cost of attendance. Second, realized revenues arenetof scholarships or discounts that the school provides to the student, because IPEDS records these as contra-revenue entries. Finally, revenues per FTE are directly comparable to spending per FTE, which will be the basis of our estimates of marginal costs. For all of these reasons, we will generally think of a college’s “price” in terms of the contribution of a marginal FTE to the college’s realized (noninvestment) revenues. That said,Figure 2highlights that the two notions of price are quite similar at the median.

Accounting issues in IPEDS are discussed in greater detail in AppendixB.
article.3. Initial Evidence on College Revenues:

In our natural experiment, we will retain only the award years from 2007 to 2013 as a window around the standards tightening, which occurred at the beginning of the 2011 award year. For consistency, we use the same time period for the analysis in this section. In columns 1 and 2 of panel A, we regress colleges’ revenues (net of investment income) and expenses on their FTEs of undergraduate instruction. We find that one additional FTE is associated with $20,280 of additional revenues, close to the median tuition of $22,890 reported inTable 1, and with $18,380 of spending. We cannot reject the hypothesis that these marginal revenue and spending numbers are equal to each other, which at first glance suggests that the typical college may not charge a very large markup over the marginal cost of enrolling each student.

In columns 3 and 4 of panel A, we repeat the same regressions with all variables in logarithms. In both cases, the estimated coefficient is above one, suggesting that average revenues and costs are both increasing in enrollment, as in the selection-based model. This follows from the identity|$\frac{d \ln Y}{d \ln X} = \frac{d Y}{d X} \big/ \frac{Y}{X}$|⁠. A coefficient greater (less) than one indicates that marginal costs are greater (less) than average costs, so average costs are increasing (decreasing) in enrollment.

Our analysis will mostly focus on the logarithm of the specifications, as in columns 3 and 4, for two reasons: First, they have the natural interpretation described just above. Second, the log-transformed data are closer to normally distributed, so the logarithm of the coefficients likely come closer to estimating an equal-weighted average of heterogeneous effects.7In Section8.1, we show that our qualitative conclusions are robust to analysis in levels.

At face value, the estimates in panel A do not strongly suggest that colleges charge large markups over marginal cost. Instead, one might suspect that tuition has risen because students are increasingly expensive to educate. This reasoning echoesEpple, Romano, and Sieg (2006): they identify college cost functions by regressing spending on (a quadratic function of) enrollment, and use these regressions to estimate that markups are only 2|$\%$|to 3|$\%$|of tuition (see their table II).8

However, we can point to at least two clear reasons to suspect upward bias in the estimates from such an approach. First, all three series—enrollment, spending, and revenues—trend steadily upward for most schools in our sample, raising concerns that we might estimate spurious relationships between them. Second, fixed costs may have some unobserved heterogeneity that is correlated with enrollment. For example, schools may have committed to large fixed costs in the past that allow them to attract a larger student body today, holding fixed other factors affecting enrollment. This fixed effect would also generate upward bias in the estimated OLS coefficients.

To address both concerns, we repeat the above regressions in differences. This removes fixed effects and also transforms the regression variables to be more plausibly stationary. Panel B ofTable 2reports the results of this approach.

The regressions of college spending (columns 2 and 4) suggest a very different perspective compared to their counterparts in panel A. Column 2, the levels specification, suggests that spending on a marginal FTE of undergraduate instruction is just above $6,900, far less than the average tuition charge. Column 4, the logarithm of the specification, suggests that average spendingfallswith enrollment (i.e., marginal costs are much lower than average costs), as the estimated elasticity is far below one. These findings appear more consistent with market power than selection effects.

However, the differenced regressions face their own identification concern, in the form of simultaneity. Spending and revenues are jointly determined with enrollment, and time-varying unobserved factors may push them in opposite directions. As just one example, in years when the economy underperforms, a school’s investment income and revenues will fall, forcing it to cut spending; but at the same time, more students likely enroll, due to a tight labor market. Because of this negative within-school correlation between the explanatory variables and regression residuals, the coefficients in panel B are likely to underestimate both the cost of educating a marginal student and the effective price paid by that student.

Indeed, the revenue regressions (columns 1 and 3) suggest that this is a serious concern. At face value, column 1 suggests that the average school received only $8,923 in revenue from students who enrolled during the sample window, which is far below the advertised tuition levels or the average realized revenues per student. Column 3 suggests that the marginal student driving enrollment growth during this time paid only 56|$\%$|of the average at their school. These magnitudes are implausible, and instead raise concerns about simultaneity in the regression.9

In the rest of the paper, we introduce an empirical strategy to address the simultaneity concerns with the differenced regressions in panel B. We seek out a demand shock that affects the growth rate of spending and revenues only through the growth rate of enrollment. The demand shock that we exploit is the sudden disqualification of a large population of students from PLUS loans.
article.4. PLUS Standards Tightening:
4.1 The PLUS program

The Parent Loans for Undergraduate Students (PLUS) program provides loans to parents of dependent undergraduate students who are unable to cover the cost of college attendance even after exhausting other forms of federal aid.Figure 3shows that aggregate PLUS borrowing has been roughly constant in dollar terms, and increasing as a fraction of total federal loan borrowing. As of the end of 2020–2021 award year, PLUS loans represented more than 24|$\%$|of the total federal undergraduate dollar loan volume, and there were 3.6 million loan recipients with $103.6 billion in parent PLUS loans outstanding.10

Unlike other federal student loan programs, the PLUS program imposes no aggregate borrowing limit, other than the cost of attendance reported by the institution (less any other financial aid). Hence, the PLUS program is a likely setting for student aid to affect college pricing.

Indeed,Figure 4shows that the average amount borrowed under the PLUS program has steadily increased since 2010, while average Stafford borrowing stayed roughly constant at the limits imposed by those programs. The growth of PLUS borrowing matches the 4|$\%$|average growth rate of tuition in our sample of private colleges over the same time period, which clearly suggests a connection between the PLUS program and the Bennett hypothesis.

Years on the horizontal axis refer to award years, as defined by the Department of Education, which begin on July 1 of the calendar year listed and end on June 30 of the following calendar year. Open in new tab Download slide

However, and again unlike other forms of aid, PLUS loans are limited along the extensive margin. Because PLUS loans are in parents’ names, and cannot be transferred to the student beneficiary, they are not made to parents with an “adverse credit history.” Our study focuses on a redefinition of this term in 2011.

4.2 The 2011 tightening of PLUS credit standards

The redefinition stemmed from the 2010 consolidation of the older Federal Family Education Loan (FFEL) program with the newer Direct Loan (DL) program. During the consolidation process, the DOE discovered that the two programs had used different interpretations of the term “adverse credit history,” a disqualifying condition that includes many types of default or delinquency. The main difference was that the (stricter) FFEL interpretation disqualified a parent if they had exhibited a 90-day or more delinquency on any debt at any point during a 5-year lookback period, whereas the DL definition only used a 90-day lookback period (Kantrowitz 2009).

Needing to harmonize the language, the Department chose the stricter FFEL definition beginning in the 2011–2012 award year. The change took effect without any public announcement from the Department of Education (Nelson 2012). AWashington Postarticle described the events:

At the time, federal officials considered the matter routine. They tightened the screening process for loan applications to ensure that certain kinds of unpaid debts were considered in a review of a parent’s credit record. That made it more likely that some applicants would be deemed to have an “adverse credit history” and therefore ineligible. Acting Deputy Education Secretary Jim Shelton said the action was taken by “middle management” officials in an effort to fix what they saw as “a glitch in the system.” He said that top officials did not review the decision before it was implemented, but that the department stood by it as consistent with laws and regulations.Anderson (2013)

The change affected a significant fraction of PLUS applicants:Goldrick-Rab, Kelchen, and Houle (2014)estimate that PLUS denial rates grew from 22|$\%$|to 42|$\%$|between 2010 and 2012. These numbers track closely withKantrowitz (2009), who estimates that (before the standards tightening) PLUS denial rates were 42|$\%$|in the FFEL program but only 21|$\%$|in the DL program.11

Colleges nationwide soon blamed the change for shortfalls in enrollment and revenues.12Several possible mechanisms could underlie such an effect. Most obviously are those parents who would have used PLUS loans but were disqualified and could not find any alternative means of financing their children’s attendance. However, some students would have been deterred from enrolling in college when their parents were denied PLUS loans, even though they would not have used those loans in any case.

This second mechanism is plausible because colleges often include PLUS loans as part of the financial aid letter with little accompanying information, and many parents know little about the program until after they are approved and the terms are presented (Burd et al. 2018). Parents may realize, after seeing these terms, that the PLUS loan is actually not the best available option for financing college. There is no available data on how often parents decline PLUS loans, but it is common enough that financial aid websites often include instructions.13

Based on this, we expect some parents would have never used PLUS loans, yet would still have received notification of a denial. This could have deterred their children’s enrollment in college for several reasons. First, a loan denial may have encouraged families to reconsider whether to take out such a large debt burden (especially if they assumed, incorrectly, that the loan approval process considered their ability to repay). Second, the application itself creates a “hard inquiry” on the parent’s credit report that might make it more difficult to obtain private credit.14

Hence, the standards tightening is best understood as a demand shock that originated from the workings of the financial aid system, but operated through several mechanisms, not just a change in realized loan volume. The important thing for our purposes is that all these mechanisms operated entirely through the demand side of the market for higher education. This makes the standards tightening a useful event to address the identification concerns with our earlier OLS results.15

In the following sections, we validate the importance of this event by sorting colleges on their exposure to it and correlating this exposure with trends in PLUS usage, enrollment, and revenues. Then we use these findings to estimate colleges’ marginal costs and markups.

Starting with the 2014–2015 school year, in response to ongoing criticism, the Department of Education began to loosen PLUS standards again. We therefore end our empirical analysis with the 2013–2014 school year.
article.5. Empirical Strategy:
5.1 Cross-sectional treatment intensity

To examine the real impact of the standards tightening in the PLUS program, we sort colleges on a proxy for the expected impact of the event. Ideally, we would like to know the fraction of students at each college whose parents would have qualified under the old credit history regime, but not under the new one. This would require a student-level match between college choice, financial aid, and parents’ credit histories. Such a data set does not exist anywhere, even confidentially, as we confirmed after filing a request with the DOE under the Freedom of Information Act.

Instead, we construct a proxy for exposure based on the IPEDS data. Our proxy is a ratio|$\text{F}_{i,2010}$|that measures the fraction of federal aid recipients at college|$i$|who came from low-income households as of 2010. Parental income, as a general proxy for credit access, is a better indicator than actual PLUS usage rates of the importance of the credit standards tightening event: First, as noted in the previous section, low-income parents could have been deterred by PLUS denials, even if they do not appear in PLUS loan statistics. Conversely, high-income parents may use PLUS loans, even if they are unlikely to have been affected by the standards tightening.

To construct|$\text{F}_{i,2010}$|⁠, we consult the campus-specific IPEDS Student Financial Aid form, which is available between 2008 and 2013. It reports the number of newly enrolled undergraduates that received any federal student aid under Title IV, referred to as “group 4,” and further breaks down this number into brackets of annual household income, of which the lowest two brackets are $0–$30,0000 and $30,000–$48,000. For each campus, we record both the total number of group 4 students and the numbers that come from these two brackets. We sum both these numbers across each campus in a college system, then calculate their ratio as our value of|$\text{F}_{i,2010}$|⁠.
article.6. Results:
6.1 Impacts on PLUS usage, enrollment, revenues, and spending

In this section, we quantify the effects of the standards tightening in a regression framework. We employ the following specification at the school-year level: $$
\begin{equation}
\Delta y_{it} \,\, = \,\, \phi_t \,\, + \,\, \gamma_t \times \text{F}_{i,2010} + \,\, \varepsilon_{it},
\label{DD_time_varying}
\end{equation}$$ (1) where |$i$| indexes schools, |$t \in [2007, 2013]$|⁠ , |$\phi_t$| is a year fixed effect, and |$\text{F}_{i,2010}$| is the fraction of the school’s students in 2010 who came from households with income below $48,000. The outcomes for |$\Delta y_{it}$| will measure, for example, school-level growth rates of PLUS loan volumes, enrollment, tuition revenues, and expenses. We cluster standard errors by school system. 16

We model the outcome variables in growth rates rather than levels due to the nonstationary growth of enrollment, revenues, and spending during the sample period (as displayed earlier inFigure 1). The coefficients|$\gamma_t$|capture the causal effect of the credit standards tightening, granted the identifying assumption of parallel trends in the growth rates of|$y_{it}$|for more and less exposed schools. Here, we confirm that the dynamic effects are consistent with this assumption.

InFigure 5, we estimate specification (1) setting|$\Delta y_{it}$|equal to the logarithm of the growth rate of the school’s PLUS recipients. The year 2010 is omitted as the reference year for|$\gamma_t$|⁠. Hence, each|$\gamma_t$|measures whether the relationship between treatment exposure and PLUS growth rate is very different in year|$t$|than in year 2010. The dashed lines in the figure are 2.6 standard errors above and below the|$\gamma_t$|estimates, representing a 99|$\%$|confidence interval.

It does not capture parents who would not have used PLUS loans in any case, but chose not to enroll their children after being denied, because these parents would have had no observable impact on PLUS volume. For this reason, the enrollment effects inTable 4are bigger than one would predict based only on the PLUS effects inTable 3.

6.2 Estimates of marginal costs

The earlier results show that the PLUS standards tightening causally decreased enrollment, revenues, and expenses for many colleges. Now we use these results to obtain instrumental variables estimates of colleges’ marginal costs of enrolling the students who departed due to this event. Intuitively, this just means dividing the “reduced form” effects on expenses, from Table 5 , by the “first stage” effect on enrollment, from Table 4 . More formally, the system of equations we estimate is: $$
\begin{align}
\Delta \ln( q_{it} ) &= \alpha + \gamma \times \text{F}_{i,2010} \times \text{After}_t + \phi \times \text{After}_t + \delta \times \text{F}_{i,2010} + \varepsilon_{it},
\label{IV_first_stage}
\end{align}$$ (4) $$
\begin{align}
\Delta \ln( c_{it} ) &= \zeta + \eta \times \Delta \ln( q_{it} )
+ \lambda \times \text{After}_t + \kappa \times \text{F}_{i,2010} + u_{it}.
\label{IV_structural_equation}
\end{align}$$ (5)

In specification (4), the first stage, we exploit the standards tightening as a shock to enrollment growth|$\Delta \ln( q_{it} )$|⁠. In (5), the structural equation, the outcome variable is the growth rate in the college’s spending|$\Delta \ln( c_{it} )$|⁠, and the key parameter of interest is|$\eta$|⁠. This parameter captures how the growth rate of college spending depends on the growth rate in enrollment. We can use it to calculate the college’s marginal cost of enrolling the students affected by the credit standards tightening, provided we assume the exclusion restriction: that|$\text{F}_{i,2010}$|only predicts changes in spending growth post-2010 due to its effect on growth in student enrollments.18

In reality, all the effects in the above equations are likely to be heterogeneous, so we must also impose a further assumption of monotonicity. Monotonicity requires that a larger share of low-income students|$\text{F}_{i,2010}$|implies a weakly larger drop in enrollment growth|$\Delta q_{it}$|after the standards tightening at every college. SeeAngrist, Graddy, and Imbens (2000)for a precise statement. The assumption is plausible: We have no reason to expect that a low-income student at any college ismorelikely to attend after PLUS loan standards tighten, holding fixed the characteristics of the school (such as price and location) that attracted low-income students in the first place.

With the monotonicity assumption,|$\eta$|can be interpreted as a local average treatment effect (LATE), where the weighting on each individual college’s effect skews toward those with the greatest first stage effect, that is, the schools whose enrollment is most sensitive to changes in student loan credit standards. SeeAngrist, Graddy, and Imbens (2000)for a precise formulation of the LATE weighting scheme. These schools are, by construction, also highly relevant to policy questions about the expansion or design of student aid. This is a key advantage of our strategy: There might be other instruments that would help identify colleges’ cost functions, but ours puts the greatest weighting on schools that matter for policy discussions about student aid programs, precisely because it originates from an adjustment to one of those programs.

This is because some students may respond to the credit standards tightening by decreasing credit hours without actually dropping out, which affects FTEs and spending, but not headcount. 6.3 Estimates of markups

With our estimates of marginal costs from the prior section, we can attempt to quantify the markups that college charge over that marginal cost. This first requires us to confront the question of how to measure the “price” paid by the marginal student affected by our natural experiment.

One obvious starting point would be to use the college’s advertised tuition and fees. InTable 1, advertised tuition and fees for the median college in our sample were $22,890 in 2010. Compared with the marginal cost of $14,000 that we estimated above, this would represent a markup of 39|$\%$|⁠.

Advertised tuition has several weaknesses as a measure of price. First, colleges engage in heavy price discrimination through discounts and institutional scholarships, such that the marginal student in our experiment might pay tuition far below the advertised amount. Second, students generate other revenue streams for the college, outside of tuition and fees, that are just as important in thinking about the Bennett hypothesis.19

To address these issues, our preferred measure of price is the college’s reported revenues in IPEDS (excluding investment income), scaled by the college’s undergraduate FTEs. By using realized revenues, we address both issues highlighted above: First, we net out any tuition discounts or scholarships from the college itself, as these are contra-revenue entries in IPEDS. Second, revenue categories in IPEDS include not only tuition but also the auxiliary services mentioned above.20

As it turns out, the median price measured in this way is quite close to the median advertised tuition in our sample, and thus generates a similar magnitude of markup: Table 1 reports that the median college in our sample reported $23,954 of revenues per FTE in 2010 (excluding investment income). Compared with our estimated marginal cost of $14,000, we would conclude that roughly 42|$\%$|of revenues per FTE represent a markup over marginal cost.

The main concern with this approach to measuring price is that some components of reported revenues may be independent of undergraduate enrollment (e.g., revenues from an attached hospital). If these revenue streams are large, then our approach may cast too wide a net, and overstate the average student’s price and the markup they paid. However, we show in the Appendix (Table B1) that such revenue streams are negligible for the average college in our sample.21Our calculations also focus on the median college, to further avoid the influence of the small number of colleges in the sample that do obtain large revenues from nonstudent sources, such as hospitals.

One also might be concerned that the marginal student affected by our experiment could have generated very different revenues than the average student at their college. However, our results so far suggest this was not the case, as the enrollment and revenue coefficients fromTables 4and5are very close (⁠|$-0.295$|and|$-0.301$|⁠, respectively). This pattern suggests that realized revenues changed proportionally to enrollment in response to our shock, consistent with a situation where affected and unaffected students paid similar average prices (i.e., generated similar revenues).

They also suggest that our identification strategy has successfully addressed concerns about downward bias in the coefficients fromTable 2, panel B.

The results in this section are our first well-identified evidence on the causes of the Bennett hypothesis. In contrast to our initial descriptive analysis, they strongly support the market power explanation over selection effects, as a markup of 40|$\%$|is very large.22As discussed in Section1, market power raises concerns about the incidence of student loan subsidies: only a fraction of the resources spent on these programs will actually benefit students. Next, we attempt to quantify the magnitude of this problem.

6.4 Quantifying incidence concerns

We will use the following formula to quantify the incidence of student loan subsidies: $$
\begin{equation}
I \quad = \quad \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{1-\beta} \,\, - \,\, (1-\theta)}\,.
\label{eqn:incidence_rearranged}
\end{equation}$$ (6)

This formula is derived in Equation (C2) in the Appendix, based on a result fromWeyl and Fabinger (2013, p. 548). It describes the incidence of a subsidy, in a market that may feature imperfect competition as well as selection effects (nonconstant marginal costs).

On the left side of (6),|$I$|is the ratio of the increase in consumer surplus, to the increase in producer surplus, that is created by introducing a small subsidy. In our context, students are the consumers, and colleges are the producers. On the right side,|$\beta$|is the Bennett hypothesis pass-through effect, defined as the change in tuition in response to a change in subsidy.

The last parameter in the formula,|$\theta$|is an elasticity-adjusted markup, defined as|$\frac{P-MC}{P}\times\epsilon_D$|⁠, where|$P$|is the cost of attending college for the marginal student,|$MC$|is the cost that student imposes on the college by attending, and|$\epsilon_D$|is the elasticity of demand for college attendance.

Based on our discussion of magnitudes in the prior section, the markup|$\frac{P-MC}{P}$|is approximately equal to 0.4. The other terms in (6) are not directly estimated by our results. However, we can make conservative estimates of these values based on prior research and intuition.

First, demand elasticity|$\epsilon_D$|is likely to be greater than one, given that colleges are differentiated products.23Combining|$\frac{P-MC}{P} \approx 0.4$|and|$\epsilon_D > 1$|suggests that|$\theta > 0.4$|⁠.

We can quantify|$\beta$|from evidence on the Bennett hypothesis. The precise interpretation of|$\beta$|is a dollar elasticity of tuition with respect topublic resourcesspent on subsidizing college attendance. Therefore, the most useful evidence on|$\beta$|is from research that focuses on grant programs. An elasticity of tuition with respect to a grant amount is directly comparable to|$\beta$|⁠.24

The grant program that has attracted the most research is the Pell program, so we survey this literature to understand realistic values for|$\beta$|⁠. This literature suggests a large range of values for|$\beta$|at private colleges in the United States. At one extreme,Singell and Stone (2007)estimate that “increases in Pell Grants appear to be matched nearly one for one by increases in list (and net) tuition,” suggesting|$\beta = 1$|⁠. At the other extreme,Turner (2017)estimates that “students’ effective prices fall by 81 cents for a given dollar of Pell Grant aid,” suggesting|$\beta = 0.19$|⁠.

Given the wide range in these estimates, we do not take a stand on any one value of|$\beta$|⁠. Instead, inFigure 9, we consider the implications of a wide range of|$\beta$|values. The figure plots the ratio|$\frac{I}{I+1}$|as a function of|$\beta$|⁠. Recall that|$I$|is the ratio of consumer surplus to producer surplus created by a subsidy. Hence, the ratio|$\frac{I}{I+1}$|can be interpreted as the fraction that students receive out of the total (consumer plus producer) surplus created by the subsidy. The figure computes this ratio for various values of|$\beta$|(plotted on the horizontal axis), fixing|$\theta = 0.4$|based on our earlier findings.

Figure 9 Subsidy incidence for different parameter values This figure plots the fraction of total surplus created by a subsidy that accrues to students, as a function of the pass-through effect of the subsidy on prices, and using our empirical analysis to inform the value of markups. More precisely, the figure plots |$I/(I+1)$|⁠ , where |$I$| is the ratio of consumer surplus to producer surplus created by the subsidy. The quantity |$I$| is calculated following Equation ( 6 ) presented in Section 6.4 of the paper. That formula depends on two parameters: |$\theta$|⁠ , an elasticity-adjusted markup that we calibrate to 0.4 based on our empirical analysis; and |$\beta$|⁠ , the pass-through effect of the subsidy on prices. We plot a range of possible values of |$\beta$| to accommodate the wide range of estimates present in the literature. See the further discussion in Section 6.4 . Open in new tab Download slide

As|$\beta$|approaches one inFigure 9, the fraction that benefits students from a marginal dollar of subsidy falls to zero. Even as|$\beta$|falls to 0.2, at the low end of the values suggested by the empirical literature, we have|$\frac{I}{I+1}\approx0.6$|⁠; that is, students receive only 60 cents of each dollar of subsidy.

This calculation confirms the fears implicit in most discussions of the Bennett hypothesis to date: a large fraction of the resources expended on subsidizing college attendance fails to benefit students in reality. In the final section of the paper, we further discuss how exactly to interpret this finding and what, if anything, can be done about its underlying causes.
article.7. Discussion:
We conclude that students receive surprisingly little of the benefit from loan subsidies. A natural question is, who receives the rest of that subsidy? From an accounting perspective, most colleges in our sample are nonprofits, and these colleges have no stakeholders designated as residual claimants in the accounting date. Indeed, our earlier analysis suggests that colleges consistently spend all their tuition revenue (causing average spending to be close to average revenues), rather than accumulating those revenues in any account that would be comparable to retained earnings.

Still, economic profits can flow through colleges to nonstudent stakeholders, in particular the various employees of the college. If those stakeholders have market power over colleges in their respective labor markets, then colleges might well be forced to bid up their compensation, and to finance the increase with higher tuition, exploiting the college’s own market power over students in the education market. While we leave a formal model for future research, this explanation would reconcile how tuition can include large markups, even as colleges themselves often appear to operate on thin margins and face significant cost pressures.

Next, the most important question raised by our findings is whether anything can be done about them. Is there an obvious way to redirect the benefits of student loan programs back to students themselves, or must we accept the situation? Again, a full answer is left to future research. However, we can offer some ideas, based on the general insight that economic policy should focus as directly as possible at addressing underlying market failures.

Market power cannot be sustained if there is free entry. To the extent that the entry costs of opening a college are large, in principle the government could attempt to pay those costs, rather than subsidizing incumbent colleges. New entry not only expands enrollment directly but also erodes the pricing power of those incumbents. In AppendixD, we present a simple model that confirms this intuition: When demand is very inelastic, it is very expensive to achieve any given increase in quantity by subsidizing students individually, due to the incidence problem highlighted by our earlier calculations. In this case, an entry subsidy may be preferable, by helping to push the market toward a more competitive structure.

The most obvious way to subsidize entry is to open or expand public college systems. Our analysis suggests this as an alternative to loan programs in areas where markups are high and demand for education is very inelastic. Recent proposals to expand the community college system may partially fit this description, as these institutions may be able to compete on price with larger 4-year institutions.25

8.3 Ruling out substitution with Pell Grants

Pell Grant maximums grew in the years surrounding the PLUS standards tightening, and there is likely a large degree of overlap between the recipients of PLUS loans and Pell Grants. Thus, when we show a decrease in PLUS uptake around the time of the standards tightening (inTable 3, andFigures 5and6), one might wonder whether this partially reflects a simple substitution away from PLUS loans and toward Pell Grants. InTable 12, we show that this pattern does not explain our results. The table repeats the specifications in columns 1 and 2 ofTable 3, but uses the volume and number of Pell Grants instead of PLUS loans as the outcome variable. If our main findings were driven by substitution between the programs, we would expect to find positive coefficients for the interaction term of interest (offsetting the negative coefficients inTable 3). Instead, Pell Grants also exhibited a negative response to the standards tightening, though somewhat smaller than the magnitudes that we found for PLUS loans inTable 3. The likely explanation is that many of the students who were deterred from enrollment due to the PLUS standards tightening would also have received Pell Grants, had they enrolled.26

8.4 Separating for-profit and nonprofit schools

Our discussion so far pools for-profit and nonprofit private colleges together into one group. One might wonder whether they exhibit different results in our analysis. In this section, we repeat our main analysis separately for these two groups.

In columns 3 and 4 ofTable 16, we replace spending with revenues as the outcome variable. At for-profit schools, the elasticity of revenue with respect to enrollment is indistinguishable from one. At nonprofit schools, this elasticity is only 0.803. Although the difference from one is still not statistically significant, at face value this coefficient suggests that students who dropped out of anonprofitcollege due to the PLUS standards tightening may have generated slightly less revenue than the average student at these colleges (i.e., colleges charged them a lower effective price). We will take this estimate into account when computing markups.

To compute those markups, we apply the same calculations as in the main analysis. At the median nonprofit college, we estimate that the students affected by the standards tightening imposed marginal costs of $9,558 (⁠|$=0.368\times\$25,974$|⁠), and generated marginal revenues of $21,574 (⁠|$=0.803\times\$26,867$|⁠), suggesting a markup of 55|$\%$|⁠. At for-profit colleges, the comparable numbers are a marginal cost of $6,318 (⁠|$=0.697\times\$9,064$|⁠), marginal revenue of $11,187 (⁠|$=1.042\times\$10,736$|⁠), and a markup of 44|$\%$|⁠. Both are close to the markup magnitude that we estimated in the main paper.

To summarize the findings in this section, we do find some heterogeneity in the reduced-form effects of the standards tightening: For-profit colleges generally experienced a larger proportional shock to their enrollment, revenues, and spending, compared to nonprofits. This is consistent with anecdotal discussions of the standards tightening (e.g.,Fishman 2014). However, the markups implied by these effects are fairly similar across the two types of school: we estimate a markup of 55|$\%$|at the median nonprofit college, and of 44|$\%$|at the median for-profit. The results suggest that the average college has significant pricing power over its students, and exploits it, regardless of its for-profit or nonprofit status.
article.9. Conclusion:
We present novel evidence for concerns about the incidence of student aid. We build on prior evidence of pass-through effects on tuition (the Bennett hypothesis), and attempt to explain this pattern by estimating colleges’ marginal costs compared to their tuition charges. For identification, we exploit the 2011 tightening of credit standards for PLUS loans. We estimate that colleges’ tuition charges reflected very large markups over cost. We thus provide a natural explanation for the Bennett hypothesis that also supports concerns about the incidence of student aid. Our analysis suggests some alternatives to subsidized loan programs that might better benefit students. More broadly, we contribute to the literature that seeks to understand the economics and market structure of higher education in the United States.

Appendix A. Aggregating and Merging IPEDS and Title IV

In the IPEDS data, the unique institutional identifier is the UnitID variable. In the Title IV data, the unique identifier is an eight-digit code labeled OPEID (for Office of Postsecondary Education ID), which is also included in IPEDS. The first six digits of the OPEID identify the participation agreement between the school and federal financial aid programs, and the final two digits identify the specific college or branch within a given agreement. The primary college within a plan is typically recorded with an OPEID ending in “00.” The vast majority of plans have only one participant college.

Title IV data are only reported by participation agreement, that is, by the first six digits of OPEID. In principle, IPEDS data are reported separately for each eight-digit OPEID identifier, but in practice many schools seem to report financial data on a combined basis (i.e., the primary campus will report the data aggregated across all other campuses in the plan, and the other campuses will report missing observations.). Hence, we aggregate all data by the first six digits of the OPEID.27In doing so, we average sticker tuition across each participant college weighting by enrollment, sum all enrollment and financial numbers across participants, and classify for-profit status and state of location according to the primary campus. In our final regression sample, about 90|$\%$|of observations are from plans with only one participant, and our general conclusions are unchanged if we use only these observations.

The Title IV Program Volume Reports provide the number of recipients and volume by loan program for each school participating in the Title IV programs. The data are available annually from award years 1999-2000 to 2005-2006. From 2006-2007 award year onward, the data are reported quarterly and we use cumulative measures through Q4. Prior to the award year 2009–2010, the Title IV data include information on both FFEL and DL programs. As mentioned above, after the elimination of the FFEL program in 2010, only the DL program is available. Thus, from award year 2010–2011 onward, the Title IV Reports only include the volume and number of recipients for the DL program. The annual data are available from award years 1999–2000 to 2005–2006.

Appendix B. IPEDS Accounting Overview

In this section, we summarize information about IPEDS accounting standards that is relevant for our analysis.28A full glossary of IPEDS terms can be found on the NCES website.29

At for-profit colleges, similar to any for-profit corporation, any excess of total revenues over total expenses is recorded as an increase in the college’s equity. At nonprofit colleges, the equity account is replaced by a “net assets” account that respects the same identity.

$$\Delta\,\text{equity (or net assets)}\ =\ \text{total revenues} - \text{total expenses}$$

Within total revenues, it is useful to separate out investment income (return on endowment). Restating the identity above, we can say that total revenues excluding investment income, minus total expenses, must equal the college’s increase in equity or net assets, minus its investment return: $$
\begin{align*}
& \Delta\,\text{equity (or net assets)} - \text{investment income}\\
&\quad=\ \text{total revenues excluding investment income} - \text{total expenses}
\end{align*}$$

InFigure B1, we aggregate and plot each of these four categories for private colleges in IPEDS in each year from 2002 to 2018. Panel A plots aggregate total revenues excluding investment income, and aggregate total expenses.30Panel B plots aggregate investment return, and the aggregate increase in equity (at for-profit colleges) or net assets (at nonprofits).

Figure B1 Sources of fluctuations in college net worth over time Both figures report aggregate numbers from IPEDS for the award years 2002–2018, excluding public colleges. “Equity or net assets” is equal to year-end total equity at for-profit colleges and year-end total net assets at nonprofit colleges. IPEDS accounting identities require that the differences between the pairs of lines in each figure must match. More precisely, for any given year, total revenues (net of investment return) minus total expenses must equal the change in aggregate equity (or net assets) minus the aggregate investment return. The first panel shows that noninvestment revenues track closely with spending, and both have been growing steadily during the years shown. The second panel reveals that aggregate investment revenues are very large and volatile compared to colleges’ other revenues and spending. However, this volatility is essentially all absorbed into changes in colleges’ equity (or net assets), and has little impact on colleges’ spending from year to year.

Table B1reveals that the average tuition share of total (noninvestment) revenue is about 71|$\%$|⁠. The next most significant revenue sources for the average college are auxiliary services (such as residence or dining halls), and federal and private grants (which may partially reflect tuition payments; see the discussion below). However, every revenue category other than tuition has a median share of zero in total revenues, and a mean share of less than 7|$\%$|⁠. On the expense side, over 90|$\%$|of total spending is on instructional, student service, support, and auxiliary enterprises.

Next, we highlight a few accounting details that help to explain these patterns in the data.

First, it may seem surprising that “net grant aid” is a negligible expense category inFigure B2andTable B1, when we know that colleges engage in heavy price discrimination through scholarships and tuition discounts.31The reason is that grants or discounts from the college to a student are a contra-revenue entry in IPEDS, lowering the school’s reported revenue from the category to which the funds are applied (typically tuition or an auxiliary enterprise, such as residence halls). In other words, the reported revenue figures already net out the effects of price discrimination. The “net grant aid” expense category only includes scholarshipsabovethe total charges applied by the college, which are then refunded to the student. These are small in the aggregate.

Second, while tuition and fees are the most important category of reported revenues by far, it does not follow that we should focus only on this category in our analysis. Residence, dining, and other auxiliary enterprises can contribute significantly to the student’s cost of attendance and the college’s revenues. Furthermore, revenues from government and private sources likely include significant amounts of student-level payments that actually went toward tuition or other attendance costs.32For these reasons, when students leave college in response to our natural experiment, this will likely have the biggest impact on revenue from tuition and fees, but may also affect other revenue categories in ways that are important to capture.

In the main paper, for the sake of simplicity, we focus on total revenues (excluding investment income) and total spending as our outcome variables. To the extent that we have strong exogenous variation in enrollment (which is the goal of our natural experiment), our regressions will identify the marginal student-level component of all revenues and expenses, while any components that are fixed, or are unrelated to undergraduate education, will be absorbed into the intercepts.

We also consider robustness to other approaches, such as using narrower categories of revenues and spending, or studying the college’s advertised sticker price. We generally find that these various approaches generate similar qualitative conclusions from our analysis. For this reason, beyond the discussion in this section, we do not attempt any deeper analysis of the ideal approach to cost and revenue accounting in higher education, which is a persistent topic of debate.33

Appendix C. Competing Models of Pass-Through and Incidence

C.1 Pass-Through Effects of a Subsidy

In this section, we explain how both of the models outlined in Section1generate the pass-through effect behind the Bennett hypothesis. To aid the discussion, we will refer repeatedly to a visual illustration in figure 8 ofCabral, Geruso, and Mahoney (2018). Panel A of that figure depicts the selection-based model, and Panel B depicts the market-power model.

We first clarify one potential source of confusion. The Bennett hypothesis is usually described as a “pass-through” effect of student loan subsidies on the college’s advertised tuition. However, in the theory literature, by convention “pass-through” refers to the change in the price paid by the consumer. These are simply mirror images: The advertised price (e.g., tuition) is gross of any subsidy provided to consumers (student borrowers), while the effective price that they pay is net of that subsidy. Hence, a positive pass-through effect of a subsidy on advertised tuition (the Bennett hypothesis) is equivalent to anincompletepass-through effect on the effective price paid.

First, we consider the pass-through effect of a subsidy (modeled as a shift in the cost curve) in a competitive market. By competitive market, we mean that the industry operates at zero profits, so equilibrium occurs at the intersection of demand and the average cost curves. When there is no selection, firms face a horizontal average cost curve. A subsidy translates one-for-one into a reduction in the price paid by the consumer, and hence no change in the advertised price. This effect is visually illustrated by the transition from point|$A$|to point|$B$|in Panel A of figure 8 inCabral, Geruso, and Mahoney (2018).

Advantageous selection means that average costs are upward sloping. In this case, a subsidy draws in marginal consumers who are more expensive than the inframarginal ones. To maintain zero profits, universities must ensure that the price paid by consumers (net of the subsidy) must fall by less than the full amount of the subsidy. The advertised price (gross of the subsidy) must therefore rise. This is the Bennett hypothesis effect. This effect is depicted by the shift from point|$A$|to point|$C$|in panel A of figure 8 inCabral, Geruso, and Mahoney (2018).

Next, we consider the pass-through effect of a subsidy on prices in a market with market power but no selection. For simplicity, we consider the extreme case of a single monopolistic producer, who sets price such that marginal revenue is equal to a constant marginal cost. When the subsidy shifts the marginal cost downward, the price paid by the consumer also falls, but by a lesser amount. This means that the advertised price gross of the subsidy will rise, again generating the Bennett hypothesis effect. Here, the intuition is that the monopolist uses her pricing power to absorb some of the subsidy as profit. This last effect is illustrated in Panel B of figure 8 fromCabral, Geruso, and Mahoney (2018): When the subsidy shifts the marginal cost downward from|$p$|to|$p'$|⁠, the equilibrium shifts from point|$C$|to point|$D$|⁠, and the price paid by the consumer falls from|$p''$|to|$p'''$|⁠.

C.2 Incidence of a Subsidy

Next, we consider the incidence of a subsidy. That is, we compare the effects of the subsidy on consumer surplus, producer surplus, and any deadweight loss.

In the selection-based model, by assumption, colleges always receive zero producer surplus, both before and after the subsidy is introduced. Then the subsidy can only increase surplus for students, not for colleges or their stakeholders. To put this differently, the selection-based model demonstrates that the Bennett hypothesis can arise even in a setting where, by construction, students are the only ones to benefit from the subsidy. In fact, the conclusion is more extreme: The marginal students who would be drawn in by a subsidy pay less than their marginal cost, so it is arguably inefficient from a social perspective to subsidize them at all.

Things are very different in the monopolistic case. Here, there is a clear case for subsidizing enrollment, due to the standard deadweight loss of market power. The problem is that a subsidy will be partially diverted into producer profits, which are positive in the presence of market power.

To illustrate this visually, refer to Panel B of figure 8 fromCabral, Geruso, and Mahoney (2018). The triangle southeast of point|$C$|⁠, and above the marginal cost curve, represents consumers who could be enrolled profitably, but are deterred by the high price set by the monopolist. Before the subsidy, consumer surplus is the triangle above the line segment|$p''{-}\,\,C$|⁠, while producer surplus is the rectangle below this segment, and above the cost curve. The subsidy shifts the equilibrium to point|$D$|⁠, and both forms of surplus grow. Since this change in total surplus is completely funded by the resources spent on the subsidy, the growth of producer surplus represents resources that are spent but do not accrue to the benefit of students.

Our IV regressions in Section6.3establish that tuition for the average college is significantly above the marginal cost of enrolling a student. Qualitatively speaking, this suggests that the market-power model seems more likely than the selection-based model as an explanation of the Bennett hypothesis. This in turn implies that concerns about the incidence of student loan subsidies should be taken seriously. Section6.4calibrates a formula that quantifies these concerns. Here, we explain how this formula is derived.

Weyl and Fabinger (2013) define the incidence |$I$| of a tax as the ratio of the decrease in consumer surplus, to the decrease in producer surplus, in response to an infinitesimal tax starting from zero. They present the following general formula (p. 548), which nests both figures above by allowing for both selection effects and any degree of imperfect competition (not necessarily monopoly): $$
\begin{align}
I \quad &= \quad \frac{\rho}{1-(1-\theta)\rho},
\end{align}$$ (C1) where |$\rho$| is the pass-through of the tax to the price paid by the consumer, and |$\theta \equiv \frac{P - MC}{P} \times \varepsilon_D$| is an elasticity-adjusted markup term. If |$I >(<) 1$| then consumers bear more (less) of the tax.

The results inWeyl and Fabinger (2013)are stated in terms of a tax, but they are easily reinterpreted in terms of a subsidy. We only need to modify the definition of|$\rho$|to maintain a positive sign: they define|$\rho \equiv \frac{dp}{d\tau}$|⁠, where|$\tau$|represents the tax, the consumer pays|$p + \tau$|⁠, and the producer receives|$p$|⁠. We define|$\rho\equiv-\frac{dp}{ds}$|⁠, where|$s$|is a subsidy, the consumer pays|$p$|⁠, and the producer receives|$p+s$|⁠. With this modification, the numerator (denominator) of the above formula can be reinterpreted as capturing theincreasein consumer (producer) surplus due to an infinitesimal increase in subsidy, evaluated at zero initial subsidy.

We can also restate their formula in terms of the pass-through effect on advertised price (gross of the subsidy), since this is the usual focus of empirical research on the Bennett hypothesis. Define advertised price as |$t \equiv p + s$| and |$\beta \equiv \frac{dt}{ds}$| as the Bennett hypothesis effect, and it is clear that |$\beta = 1-\rho$|⁠ , so we can substitute this in to obtain: $$
\begin{align}
I \quad &= \quad \frac{1}{\frac{1}{1-\beta} \,\, - \,\, (1-\theta)}
\label{eqn:incidence}
\end{align}$$ (C2)

When colleges have market power (⁠|$\theta > 0$|⁠), then the denominator of|$I$|is strictly positive. This means that some part of the subsidy goes toward producer surplus (stakeholders of the college) rather than consumer surplus (student borrowers). Taking the partial derivative of (C2) with respect to the market power parameter|$\theta$|⁠, we obtain|$\frac{\partial I}{\partial \theta} = -I^2 < 0$|⁠. Hence, where colleges have more market power, students will obtain a smaller fraction of the resources spent on a subsidy.34The main goal of our empirical strategy is to estimate|$\theta$|⁠. Then (in Section6.4of the paper) we calibrate a plausible range of values of|$I$|based on estimates of|$\beta$|from prior literature.

Appendix D. Model of Subsidies with Barriers to Entry

In this section, we consider the problem of a government intervening to expand enrollment in the market for higher education, at the minimum possible cost in terms of public funds. Consistent with our empirical analysis, we assume that markups are very large. We microfound this with the plausible assumption of large entry costs.

We allow the government to choose between a unit subsidy to each enrollee (e.g., offering students below-market interest rates on loans, as with most current programs), and an entry subsidy (e.g., directly paying the cost of opening a new school).

A single incumbent monopolist school faces a constant marginal cost of|$mc$|⁠, and a potential entrant faces an entry cost of|$C$|and the same marginal cost of|$mc$|⁠. If the entrant joins the market, the two schools will compete à la Bertrand, thus competing away all profits and attaining an efficient level of enrollment.

Left on its own, the entrant will not choose to pay the entry cost, but the government can choose to pay this cost on its behalf. Our question is, when will it be cheaper for the government to do so, as opposed to attaining the efficient level of enrollment through a unit subsidy instead? To answer this question, we calculate the amount of spending required by the latter approach.

The efficient quantity |$q^*$| is defined by |$p(q^*) = mc$|⁠ , and the monopolist sets marginal revenue |$mr(q)$| equal to |$mc$|⁠ . So, to achieve quantity |$q^*$|⁠ , one must set |$s$| such that |$mc - s = mr(q^*) = mc + q^* \times p'(q^*)$|⁠ . From this, we obtain |$s = - q^* \times p'(q^*)$|⁠ , and the total expenditures on the subsidy are |$q^* \times s = -(q^*)^2 \times p'(q^*) = - q^* \times p(q^*) \times \frac{d \ln p}{d \ln q}$|⁠ . Finally, we apply |$p(q^*) = mc$| and find that the entry subsidy is cheaper than the unit subsidy if and only if $$
\begin{equation}
C \quad < \quad \frac{ q^* \, \times \, mc} { \varepsilon_D},
\label{eqn:compare_entry_unit_subsidy}
\end{equation}$$ (D1) where |$\varepsilon_D \equiv -\frac{d \ln q}{d \ln p}$| is the price elasticity of demand.

The right side of (D1) measures the cost, in terms of public resources spent, of achieving the efficient level of enrollment by subsidizing college attendance at the student level. The formula shows that the costs of this approach are higher when (1) the optimal enrollment level|$q^*$|is high, (2) the marginal cost of enrolling a student is high, and (3) the demand elasticity for college is low.

That the price elasticity of demand for college seems to be quite low suggests that the costs of the current student-level approach to subsidizing college attendance may be quite high. More generally, when colleges have more pricing power, subsidizing college in this way is likely more expensive. (Here, pricing power is completely determined by|$\varepsilon_D$|⁠, because we focus on a monopolist.)

In contrast, an entry subsidy entails a large up-front cost, but a reduction in colleges’ market power thereafter. If the cost of subsidizing schools with market power is sufficiently high relative to this fixed entry cost, an entry subsidy may be preferable.
